parsed.summary: This commit renames the npm scope and modifies the setup command flow. The scope rename is a breaking change for existing installations that reference the old package name, and there's no migration path documented. The setup.md changes add interactive menu behavior, but as a markdown command file it cannot handle error cases or provide rollback if the user makes an incorrect selection.

findings.title: npm scope rename is a breaking change with no migration path
findings.body: The package.json and plugin.json changes rename the npm scope. Any existing users who installed the plugin under the old scope name will have a broken reference. The CHANGELOG mentions v0.4.7 but does not document a migration command or instructions for users to transition from the old scope to the new one. The old package name may still be published on npm, creating confusion and potential supply-squatting risk if not deprecated.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit migration instructions to CHANGELOG.md and release_card.md. Ensure the old npm package is deprecated with a pointer to the new scope. Verify the old scope package is either repointed or marked deprecated before shipping.

findings.title: Interactive menu in setup.md lacks guardrails for non-interactive environments
findings.body: commands/setup.md now includes an interactive menu when the plugin is already configured. If this command is executed in a non-interactive context (e.g., CI, scripted setup, or a terminal that doesn't support interactive prompts), the user will hit an unresolvable state with no documented fallback. There is no visible flag or alternative path for non-interactive reconfiguration.
findings.recommendation: Document or implement a non-interactive fallback path (e.g., a flag like --force or --reconfigure) so scripted setups don't hang or fail silently.

findings.title: marketplace.json and plugin.json references may be inconsistent after scope rename
findings.body: Both marketplace.json and plugin.json were modified in this commit alongside the package.json scope rename. If any field in these JSON files still references the old scope (e.g., icon URLs, documentation links, or internal identifiers), users may encounter broken links or failed plugin resolution after update.
findings.recommendation: Audit all string values in marketplace.json and plugin.json for residual references to the old npm scope. Verify any URLs, identifiers, or package name fields are fully updated.

next_steps: Run `git diff 7766943^..7766943 -- package.json` to confirm the exact scope rename and verify no other package references are stale.
Check if the old npm scope package exists and needs deprecation notice published.
Verify the setup.md interactive menu handles non-TTY or piped input gracefully.
Search the entire repo for any remaining references to the old scope name that were not updated in this commit.
